Train: 150 троек (item1, item2, result), test: 70 пар, candidates.csv с 70 возможными метками; все слова — короткие англоязычные фразы в нижнем регистре. Метрика — Accuracy (доля верных назначений из 70). Жёсткое ограничение: разрешён только претрейн `bert-base-uncased`, никаких других моделей и внешних данных.

## abstract

This competition is organized by the [IOAI Community Contest group](https://ioai-community-contest.netlify.app/) (not affiliated with IOAI), which is made up of IOAI alumni. It is meant to prepare students for the IOAI (International Olympiad in Artificial Intelligence).

---

## Description

## 1. Problem Description

In the game Little Alchemy, players combine elements to discover new ones. Fire and water make steam. Earth and seeds make a tree. Some combinations are intuitive, others require creativity, dinosaur plus fire becomes a dragon.

You are given a dataset of 150 training combination rules (`item1 + item2 → result`) and 70 test pairs with hidden results. Your job is to predict which result belongs to each test pair.

## 2. Dataset

```
data/
├── train.csv       # 150 rows: item1, item2, result
├── test.csv        # 70 rows: Id, item1, item2 (target hidden)
└── candidates.csv  # 70 rows: result (unique candidate labels)
```

- **Training set:** 150 labeled examples
- **Test set:** 70 pairs to predict
- **Candidates:** The list of 70 possible results for the test set
- All items are lowercase English words or short phrases
- The assignment is bijective, each test pair maps to exactly one unique candidate, and each candidate is used once.

![Alchemy Combination Example](https://imgur.com/bWlfm5A.png) 

## 3. Task

For each of the 70 test pairs, predict the correct result from the candidate list.

**Constraint:** You may only use `bert-base-uncased`, no other pretrained models or external data allowed.

---

## data-description

```
data/
├── train.csv       # 150 rows: item1, item2, result
├── test.csv        # 70 rows: Id, item1, item2
└── candidates.csv  # 70 rows: result (unique candidate labels)
```

- **Training set:** 150 labeled examples
- **Test set:** 70 pairs to predict
- **Candidates:** The list of 70 possible results for the test set
- All items are lowercase English words or short phrases
- The assignment is bijective, each test pair maps to exactly one unique candidate, and each candidate is used once.

---

## Evaluation

## 4. Submission

Submit a CSV with columns `Id` and `result`:

```python
import pandas as pd

submission = pd.DataFrame({
    'Id': test_df['Id'],
    'result': predictions
})
submission.to_csv('submission.csv', index=False)
```

## 5. Score

Accuracy: fraction of correct assignments.

$$ \text{Accuracy} = \frac{\text{Number of Correct Predictions}}{70} $$

- **Baseline Score:** ~0.08
- **Reference Solution Score:** ~0.41

The baseline can be found on the **[Code](https://www.kaggle.com/code/antoningorokva/baseline-alchemy-aicc-round-4)** section of this competition or in Google Colab [Here](https://colab.research.google.com/#fileId=https%3A//storage.googleapis.com/kaggle-colab-exported-notebooks/antoningorokva/baseline-alchemy-aicc-round-4.fedbf9a4-dcae-41e7-8d23-4fd53d64eb73.ipynb%3FX-Goog-Algorithm%3DGOOG4-RSA-SHA256%26X-Goog-Credential%3Dgcp-kaggle-com%2540kaggle-161607.iam.gserviceaccount.com/20260219/auto/storage/goog4_request%26X-Goog-Date%3D20260219T205732Z%26X-Goog-Expires%3D259200%26X-Goog-SignedHeaders%3Dhost%26X-Goog-Signature%)

---

## rules

- use of LLMs for writing code or getting task ideas is forbidden
- use of internet besides the reading of official library documentations and accessing the contest platform is forbidden
- communicating with anyone during the contest is forbidden
- For discussing competition details, participants may use the optional `#clarification-requests` channel on the [Discord server](https://discord.com/invite/7GfxrqRreY)

We can't enforce these rules, but following them ensures fair play and a more accurate simulation of the IOAI. It is in the best interest of the contestants they follow the rules.

tasks.full_description:
## abstract

This competition is organized by the **IOAI Community Contest** group (not affiliated with IOAI), which is made up of IOAI alumni. It is meant to **prepare** students for the IOAI (International Olympiad in Artificial Intelligence).

Baseline: [here](https://colab.research.google.com/drive/1lk2EPJZFZgVvusyxrbMP2lOZ9YrY8tf6?usp=sharing)

To use the baseline, copy it to your Google Drive. We will also upload soon a notebook on Kaggle's Code section

---

## Task

At the North Pole, Santa’s workshop runs a special **environmental sound system** to train the reindeer and entertain the elves while they prepare gifts. The system uses recordings of real-world environmental sounds — like wind, footsteps, and birds — to simulate different outdoor conditions in miniature training fields.

One hectic December morning, a young elf named **Jingle** accidentally spilled hot cocoa over the main audio mixer. The spill caused the recorder to **combine two separate environmental recordings into a single track**. Now, each file contains **two overlapping sounds**, making it impossible for the reindeer to train properly and for the elves to time the festive music and toy assembly correctly.

You’ve been hired as Santa’s **Audio Restoration Elf** to separate each mixed recording back into its original environmental sources, so the workshop can run smoothly for Christmas Eve.

For each mixed recording, reconstruct the **two original environmental audio sources**.

- Each input contains exactly two overlaid sounds
- The order of output signals does not matter

**Input**
  - One mixed audio waveform of length **16,000**

**Output**
  - Two reconstructed waveforms:
    - `sig_1` — length 16,000
    - `sig_2` — length 16,000
  - Values are continuous floats

Metric is **Mean Squared Error** for both sound arrays.

## Dataset

The elves organized the available data as follows:

```
data/
  train.csv
  test.csv
```

**Training Set (`train.csv`)**
  - Columns:
    - `file` — path to the mixed audio recording
    - `sig_1` — first original environmental sound
    - `sig_2` — second original environmental sound
  - Mono audio signals
  - Each signal contains exactly **16,000 samples**

**Test Set (`test.csv`)**
  - Columns:
    - `ID`
    - `file` — path to a mixed audio recording
  - Ground truth signals are not provided

### Evaluation Metric

The score is a **per-sample permutation-invariant mean squared error (MSE)**.

For each sample, two matchings are considered and the smaller error is used:  
`min( MSE(sig_1, sig_1_hat) + MSE(sig_2, sig_2_hat), MSE(sig_1, sig_2_hat) + MSE(sig_2, sig_1_hat) )`

The final score is the average of this value over all samples.  
Lower is better.

## Submission

Submit a file named **`submission.csv`**.

- Columns:
  - `ID` — must match `test.csv`
  - `sig_1` — Base85-encoded float32 array
  - `sig_2` — Base85-encoded float32 array
- Each decoded signal must contain exactly **16,000 float32 values**
- Row order must exactly match `test.csv`

Submissions not following this format will be considered invalid.

---

## data-description

**Training Set (`train.csv`)**
  - Columns:
    - `file` — path to the mixed audio recording
    - `sig_1` — first original environmental sound
    - `sig_2` — second original environmental sound
  - Mono audio signals
  - Each signal contains exactly **16,000 samples**

**Test Set (`test.csv`)**
  - Columns:
    - `ID`
    - `file` — path to a mixed audio recording
  - Ground truth signals are not provided

---

## rules

- use of LLMs for writing code or getting task ideas is forbidden
- use of internet besides the reading of official library documentations and accessing the contest platform is forbidden
- communicating with anyone during the contest is forbidden
- only ask questions regarding task statements using the `#clarification-requests` on the discord server

We can't enforce these rules, but following them ensures fair play and a more accurate simulation of the IOAI. It is in the best interest of the contestants they follow the rules.

tasks.full_description:
## abstract

This competition is organized by the **IOAI Community Contest** group (not affiliated with IOAI), which is made up of IOAI alumni.  
It is meant to prepare students for the **IOAI (International Olympiad in Artificial Intelligence)**.  
Information about the task can be found below and the `baseline.ipynb` file is in the "Data" and "Code" sections.

---

## Task

## Storyline

Ian has a problem.  

He's a hardworking student. But perhaps *too* hardworking. Every waking moment, he's either filling forms, writing essays, or drafting proposals.  

That means no time for rest. On the go, or even during meals, Ian types away on his tiny phone screen, thumbs mashing away imprecisely at the keyboard. Obviously, that won't do.

So Ian asks you for help. As an AI specialist, surely you can do something for him?  

---

## Problem Statement

Your task is to build an **autocorrect model**, to correct misspellings in text. Please note the **Architecture Restrictions** below.

---

## Input Format
- `train.csv`: This file contains your training data. We provide two columns:
  - `text`: The 'correct' text.
  - `misspell`: The text with errors.
- `test.csv`: This file contains the data to be inferenced during evaluation. Only the `misspell` column is provided.

## Output Format

Please submit a `.csv` file with **no header**, and items in the same order as in `test.csv`. Sample code is given below.

---

## Scoring Method

The performance of your model will be determined by **Character Error Rate (CER)**.  

CER is defined as `(S + D + I) / N`, where S is the number of substitutions, D is the number of deletions, I is the number of insertions, C is the number of correct characters, N is the number of characters in the reference. Thus, **a lower CER is better**, with 0 being the ideal.

The baseline solution in this notebook scores **0.0944**, rounded to four decimal places.

---

## Allowed Models / Architecture Restrictions

1. You **may not** use any pre-trained models. This includes direct use of API, fine-tuning open-source models, or any other AI model that is not trained from scratch within this notebook.
2. You **may not** train your model on the test samples in any way. This includes any unsupervised learning technique, calculating statistics over them etc.
3. Your model must run **CPU-only**, and evaluate all 2643 samples in `test.csv` within the time limit of 250s, according to the code in the evaluation section. (After all, Ian's mistyping on a phone, not a server with a T4!)

---

## Postscript by Ian

> *Psst, yo! Thanksf or helpung me witj fhis... if it hrlps i alywas **make errors in a somewhat predictable way, so try to run some statistics on my misspelled sentences!***  
> *also, sometim esmy boss makrs me dp **really really long documents**, sotry ofr rhat.*

---

## data-description

Here you can find the datasets provided to the contestants

---

## rules

- use of LLMs for writing code or getting task ideas is **forbidden**
- use of internet besides the reading of official library documentations and accessing the contest platform is **forbidden**
- communicating with anyone during the contest is **forbidden**
- only ask questions regarding task statements using the #clarification-requests on the [discord server](https://discord.com/invite/7GfxrqRreY)

We can't enforce these rules, but following them ensures fair play and a more accurate simulation of the IOAI. It is in the best interest of the contestants they follow the rules.

## abstract This competition is organized by the IOAI Community Contest group (not affiliated with IOAI), which is made up of IOAI alumni. It is meant to prepare students for the IOAI (International Olympiad in Artificial Intelligence). Information about the task can be found below and the baseline.ipynb file is in the "Data" and "Code" sections. You're an IT guy at the AICC Hospital. Many patients are waiting to get their diagnostics from their brain CT scans, however all the doctors were laid off because of AGI. The issue is GBT 6 is busy counting the strawberries in R (he says there are two 🍓s in an R), so you have to do the job yourself. You will receive images with the CT scans and a few labels generated by the AGI (they are for sure correct). To perfect your solution, you are given sample images (training dataset), and you will make the diagnostics (find the correct label) for the test dataset. Formally, your task is to classify the images of the test set into the 4 classes (no tumor + 3 tumor types). You are given a training set with roughly ~2% of the data labeled. You are allowed to use a pretrained resnet18 model, the code for using it is provided in the baseline (Code section). Restrictions: - no manually labeling - do not use any other pretrained models besides resnet18 - the run time of the notebook (including training) has to be at most 20 minutes, and it should fit within a P100 GPU (16gb vram) Baseline: [Google Drive link ](https://drive.google.com/file/d/178Ik97pl1t_dSO8eRqMAVSkoFcUzUQ05/view?usp=sharing), or go to the Code section for the pinned notebook. --- ## Description You are provided with a `train.csv`: - `image_id`: ID of the image sample, corresponding image is `train/{image_id}.jpg` - `label`: 4 classes (no tumor + 3 tumor types) Images are stored in the directories: - `train/train`: training images (~2% have labels) - `test/test`: test images to be used in submission creation --- ## data-description You are provided with a `train.csv`: - `image_id`: ID of the image sample, corresponding image is `train/{image_id}.jpg` - `label`: 4 classes (no tumor + 3 tumor types) Images are stored in the directories: - `train/train`: training images (~2% have labels) - `test/test`: test images to be used in submission creation --- ## Evaluation The evaluation metric is the Macro F1 score. ## abstract

This competition is organized by the [IOAI Community Contest group](https://ioai-community-contest.netlify.app/) (not affiliated with IOAI), which is made up of IOAI alumni. It is meant to prepare students for the IOAI (International Olympiad in Artificial Intelligence).

---

## Description

## 1. Problem description

A studio trained 250 judges to look at images and decide whether each one shows a particular visual pattern. Every judge learned exactly one of 10 patterns and answers yes or no to any image you show them. 25 judges were assigned per pattern. The roster was overwritten before a backup was made, and only 50 judges have since been re-identified. The other 200 still answer yes or no, but nobody knows which pattern they were trained on. Figure it out.

## 2. Dataset

A single file `models.pt`, a PyTorch state bundle with two keys:

- `train_models`: list of 50 dicts (5 per class), each with `seed` (int), `state_dict`, and `target_class` (int in 0..9).
- `test_models`: list of 200 dicts (20 per class, shuffled), each with `seed` and `state_dict` only.

Every classifier is a small CNN that takes a 1-channel 28x28 image with pixel values in [0, 1] and outputs a single logit. A positive logit means the classifier recognized the image as its target class; a negative logit means it did not.

The architecture depends on the `seed` field. Reconstruct each classifier with this code:

```python
import random
import torch.nn as nn

class Model(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, seed=42):
        super().__init__()
        layers = []
        in_ch = 1
        rng = random.Random(seed)
        num_blocks = rng.randint(2, 3)
        for _ in range(num_blocks):
            out_ch = rng.choice([8, 16, 32])
            k = rng.choice([3, 5])
            padding = k // 2
            layers.append(nn.Conv2d(in_ch, out_ch, kernel_size=k, padding=padding))
            layers.append(nn.ReLU())
            layers.append(nn.MaxPool2d(2))
            in_ch = out_ch
        layers.append(nn.AdaptiveAvgPool2d((1, 1)))
        self.features = nn.Sequential(*layers)
        self.classifier = nn.Linear(in_ch, 1)

    def forward(self, x):
        x = self.features(x)
        x = x.view(x.size(0), -1)
        return self.classifier(x)
```

For each dict in `train_models` or `test_models`:

```python
m = Model(seed=item['seed'])
m.load_state_dict(item['state_dict'])
m.eval()
```

## 3. Task

You are given 250 binary image classifiers. Each one was trained to recognize exactly one of 10 classes.

You know the target class for the 50 classifiers in `train_models`. The 200 classifiers in `test_models` are unlabeled. Predict the target class for each of them.

You do not have access to the original training data. No external datasets or pretrained models are allowed.

---

## data-description

## Dataset

A single file `models.pt`, a PyTorch state bundle with two keys:

- `train_models`: list of 50 dicts (5 per class), each with `seed` (int), `state_dict`, and `target_class` (int in 0..9).
- `test_models`: list of 200 dicts (20 per class, shuffled), each with `seed` and `state_dict` only.

Every classifier is a small CNN that takes a 1-channel 28x28 image with pixel values in [0, 1] and outputs a single logit. A positive logit means the classifier recognized the image as its target class; a negative logit means it did not.

The architecture depends on the `seed` field. Reconstruct each classifier with this code:

```python
import random
import torch.nn as nn

class Model(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, seed=42):
        super().__init__()
        layers = []
        in_ch = 1
        rng = random.Random(seed)
        num_blocks = rng.randint(2, 3)
        for _ in range(num_blocks):
            out_ch = rng.choice([8, 16, 32])
            k = rng.choice([3, 5])
            padding = k // 2
            layers.append(nn.Conv2d(in_ch, out_ch, kernel_size=k, padding=padding))
            layers.append(nn.ReLU())
            layers.append(nn.MaxPool2d(2))
            in_ch = out_ch
        layers.append(nn.AdaptiveAvgPool2d((1, 1)))
        self.features = nn.Sequential(*layers)
        self.classifier = nn.Linear(in_ch, 1)

    def forward(self, x):
        x = self.features(x)
        x = x.view(x.size(0), -1)
        return self.classifier(x)
```

For each dict in `train_models` or `test_models`:

```python
m = Model(seed=item['seed'])
m.load_state_dict(item['state_dict'])
m.eval()
```

---

## Evaluation

## 4. Submission

A CSV named `submission.csv` with a header and 200 rows:

```
id,class
0,3
1,7
...
199,4
```

`id` is the index of the model inside `test_models`. `class` is an integer in [0..9].

## 5. Scoring

Macro-averaged F1 over the 10 classes.

$$
F1_{\text{macro}} = \frac{1}{10} \sum_{c=0}^{9} \frac{2 \cdot \text{precision}_c \cdot \text{recall}_c}{\text{precision}_c + \text{recall}_c}
$$

Baseline solution: 0.19
Reference solution: 1.00


The baseline can be found on the **[Code](https://www.kaggle.com/code/antoningorokva/baseline-classifier-classifier-aicc-round-6)** section of this competition.

---

## rules

- use of LLMs for writing code or getting task ideas is forbidden
- use of internet besides the reading of official library documentations and accessing the contest platform is forbidden
- communicating with anyone during the contest is forbidden
- For discussing competition details, participants may use the optional `#clarification-requests` channel on the [Discord server](https://discord.com/invite/7GfxrqRreY)

We can't enforce these rules, but following them ensures fair play and a more accurate simulation of the IOAI. It is in the best interest of the contestants they follow the rules.

tasks.description: Табличная регрессия с устойчивостью к namespace-зашумлённому таргету: часть строк train испорчена «учениками», тестовая выборка содержит только чистые «учительские» записи с истинной линейной зависимостью. Train ~120 строк, 4 числовых признака feature1-feature4, целевая переменная target (numerical). Метрика — Mean Squared Error. Запрещены LLM-инструменты, внешний интернет и общение с участниками.
tasks.full_description:
## abstract This competition is organized by the **IOAI Community Contest** group (not affiliated with IOAI), which is made up of IOAI alumni. It is meant to prepare students for the **IOAI (International Olympiad in Artificial Intelligence)**. Information about the task can be found below and the `baseline.ipynb` file is in the "Data" and "Code" sections. --- ## Task # Task Description ## Problem Statement A math teacher manually created a dataset to illustrate the relationship between study effort and exam scores. Each student’s actual performance generally follows a linear trend: more effort tends to yield higher scores. However, some students **messed with the data**, introducing conflicting entries to make it appear as if higher effort doesn’t improve scores. Your task is to build a model that predicts a student’s **true expected score**, based only on the training data provided. Note that the training data contains both the teacher’s original entries and the corrupted entries added by the students. Using the provided training data, train a model that can generalize to the **teacher-only test set**, which contains only valid entries reflecting the true relationship. ## Input Format - `train.csv`: Contains the training data that participants can use to fit their models. **Columns**: - `feature1` — numerical feature - `feature2` — numerical feature - `feature3` — numerical feature - `feature4` — numerical feature - `target` — numerical target variable (student-modified) - `test.csv`: Contains the test rows that participants will make predictions for. **Columns**: - `ID` — unique identifier for each row - `feature1` — numerical feature - `feature2` — numerical feature - `feature3` — numerical feature - `feature4` — numerical feature - `solution.csv`: Hidden ground truth target values for all test rows. Participants **do not have access** to this file during the competition. **Columns**: - `ID` — matches IDs in `test.csv` - `Target` — true target value (teacher-only data) ## Output Format Participants must submit a CSV file (`submission.csv`) with the following columns: - `ID` — must match the `ID` column in `test.csv` - `Target` — predicted numerical values for the corresponding rows in `test.csv` ## Scoring Method Submissions are evaluated using the **Mean Squared Error (MSE)** between the predicted values and the teacher-only targets: $$ \text{MSE} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2 $$ Where:
  • yi — true teacher target from solution.csv
  • ŷi — predicted value from the participant submission
  • n — number of samples in the test set
Lower MSE indicates a better model. ## Allowed Models / Architecture Restrictions - Participants can use **any model**, including but not limited to: - Linear Regression - Decision Trees / Random Forests / Gradient Boosting - Neural Networks - Participants can use all four features provided in the dataset (`feature1`–`feature4`) and any preprocessing they choose. - **Participants must not use** the solution file for training or fitting (which will not be provided to them anyway). --- ## data-description Here you can find the datasets provided to the contestants and the baseline --- ## rules - use of LLMs for writing code or getting task ideas is **forbidden** - use of internet besides the reading of official library documentations and accessing the contest platform is **forbidden** - communicating with anyone during the contest is **forbidden** - only ask questions regarding task statements using the #clarification-requests on the discord server We can't enforce these rules, but following them ensures fair play and a more accurate simulation of the IOAI. It is in the best interest of the contestants they follow the rules.

tasks.description: Классификация изображений с domain shift: обучить модель классифицировать скетчи, имея размеченные только фотографии и мультяшные картинки. Train: размеченные cartoon/photograph + 9582 неразмеченных скетча; валидация — 200 размеченных скетчей; test — 4624 скетча. Метрика — F1 Score. Запрещены внешние датасеты, предобученные модели (кроме torchvision) и любое обучение/псевдоразметка по test/val.
tasks.full_description:
## abstract

This competition is organized by the **IOAI Community Contest** group (not affiliated with IOAI), which is made up of IOAI alumni. It is meant to **prepare** students for the IOAI (International Olympiad in Artificial Intelligence).

The baseline can be found on Colab [here](https://colab.research.google.com/drive/16444RatJ7G56LLnGKbzhploWcVpuA-fj?usp=sharing).

---

## Description

You are to train a model that can classify *sketches* into their respective categories.

However, **none of the sketches are labelled**. Instead, you are given labelled *photographs* and *cartoons* corresponding to the categories.

---

### Evaluation Format

You must output a CSV file with two columns:
- `filename`: the exact file name of the prediction, e.g. `xxxxxx.jpg`
- `class_name`: the exact name of the class as per the `cartoon`/`photograph` directories, e.g. `alarm_clock`

---

### Scoring Method

The performance of your model will be determined by **F1 Score**.

The baseline solution in this notebook scores **0.4093**, rounded to 4 decimal places.

---

### Architecture Restrictions

1. You **may not** use any pre-trained models, apart from those provided in [torchvision](https://docs.pytorch.org/vision/main/models.html).
2. You **may not** train your model directly on the validation or test samples in any way. This includes any unsupervised learning technique, calculating statistics over them, running GPT-5 agents etc.
3. You **may not** use any external dataset.
4. You **may not** label the unlabeled sketches or test set with any pre-trained model, e.g. CLIP.

---

## data-description

You have been provided five folders and one file:
- `cartoon`, `photograph`: these contain subfolders titled with the name of each class. Within the subfolders are photos/cartoons of that specific class. For example, `cartoon/ant/xxxxxx.jpg`.
- `sketch`: this contains a single subfolder, `unlabeled`, which contains **9582** randomized, unlabeled sketches (e.g. `sketch/unlabeled/xxxxxx.jpg`).
- `sketch_val`: this contains **200** labeled sketches of the same classes as `cartoon`/`photograph`. This is purely for validation purposes, and labels are provided in the file `val.csv`. The folder contains the files directly (e.g. `sketch_val/xxxxxx.jpg`).
- `sketch_test`: this contains **4624** sketches. You are to classify them into the classes from `cartoon`/`photograph`, without any labels. The folder contains the files directly (e.g. `sketch_val/xxxxxx.jpg`).

---

## rules

## Task-Specific Architecture Restrictions

1. You **may not** use any pre-trained models, apart from those provided in [torchvision](https://docs.pytorch.org/vision/main/models.html).
2. You **may not** train your model directly on the validation or test samples in any way. This includes any unsupervised learning technique, calculating statistics over them, running GPT-5 agents etc.
3. You **may not** use any external dataset.
4. You **may not** label the unlabeled sketches or test set with any pre-trained model, e.g. CLIP.

## AICC Contest Rules

- The use of LLMs for writing code or getting task ideas is forbidden.
- Use of the internet, besides the reading of official library documentation and accessing the contest platform, is forbidden.
- Communicating with anyone during the contest is forbidden.
- Only ask questions regarding task statements using the #clarification-requests on the discord server.

We can't enforce these rules, but following them ensures fair play and a more accurate simulation of the IOAI. It is in the best interest of the contestants they follow the rules.

---

## Storyline

Alexander and Stevie are explorers. Together, they've been on numerous adventures - sandy Bulgarian beaches, scaling the Great Wall of China...

Now, they're boarded the AI hype train, hoping to unlock unparalleled efficiency by leveraging AGI for automated, agentic scouting. No more leg-breaking work, when they can just sit back, relax, and watch the bot go.  

They've just sent their first prototype out into the wild for data collection, watching beautiful, noiseless data stream in when...

*"Hey Alex - what does that weird nondescript button do?"*  
*"Nothing much, just toggles some super secret settings. Just don't press it now since we're doing data col-*NO PLEASE DON*-"*  
*"AHH!"*

The monitor freezes.

Then - *ding!* - a flash of color. The display melts into hyper-saturated cartoons, like a Gen Alpha YouTube video on steroids.

*"..."*  
*"That wasn't too bad, right...?"*  
*"Shut up, Stevie. Let's just cycle through the other settings to get back to normal."*

Another click, and this time, the world shifts into elegant shades of penciled grey.

*"Stevie... the console hanged."*  
*"...WHAT? The prototype's in the middle of nowhere, and we can't call it back?"*  
*"Yep. I think we're cooked."*  
*"..."*

Just as despair settles in, Stevie's eyes light up.

*"Hold on... I think I can do something with the data we have so far."*

tasks.description: Cloze-style multiple choice (4 опции): выбрать предложение, лучше всего заполняющее пропуск между before/after в эссе. Train: размеченные примеры с метками 0–3, test: 80 строк без меток (фичи — before, after, opt_0..opt_3). Метрика — macro F1. Ограничения: <16 GB VRAM, время notebook <30 мин, запрещены LLM для генерации кода и внешний интернет, разрешены общедоступные pretrained-модели (CLIP, ResNet, ViT и т. п.).
tasks.full_description:
## abstract This competition is organized by the **IOAI Community Contest** group (not affiliated with IOAI), which is made up of IOAI alumni. It is meant to **prepare** students for the IOAI (International Olympiad in Artificial Intelligence). Santa’s elves were racing to finish the annual Christmas storybook for children worldwide, but one page stayed stubbornly blank. In the tale, a quiet snowfall had just begun outside the workshop windows; the next scene needed to explain how the gentle hush led the reindeer to gather at the stable door, eager for the long flight. Mrs. Claus read the four candidate sentences aloud beside the crackling fireplace: one spoke of jingling bells, another of sugar-cookie crumbs, a third of glowing northern lights, the last of a distant choir. Only the sentence that wove the stillness of the snow into the reindeers’ anticipation would let the story glide forward like a sleigh on fresh powder. The elves’ task—identical to yours—was to choose the single line that kept the narrative glow alive so the book could be wrapped and tucked into every stocking by dawn. In this task, you are given essays with a missing sentence in the middle. Your goal is to identify which of the four provided options best completes the gap. This tests your understanding of textual coherence, context, and semantic relationships between sentences. Constraints: - **Memory limit**: Solutions must run with less than 16GB VRAM - **Time limit**: The entire notebook execution must complete in under 30 minutes - **General pretrained models allowed**: You may use other general-purpose pretrained models (e.g., CLIP, ResNet, ViT, etc.) - **no code written by an LLM**: since this is an educational contest, you will win most by writing the code on your own. Do not vibe code. Baseline: https://drive.google.com/file/d/14MRVq9KsgFIinUDZobP3YqxFDDmIIYld/view?usp=sharing To use the baseline, copy it to your Google Drive. We will also upload soon a notebook on Kaggle's Code section. --- ## Description ## Dataset You have access to two files: - **train.csv**: Contains labeled examples with complete sentence mappings - **test.csv**: Contains unlabeled examples where you must predict the correct option `train.csv`: - `sampleID`: Unique identifier for each essay - `before`: Text preceding the gap (lorem ipsum in previews) - `after`: Text following the gap (lorem ipsum in previews) - `opt_0`, `opt_1`, `opt_2`, `opt_3`: Four candidate sentences to fill the gap - `label`: Correct option index (0, 1, 2, or 3) `test.csv`: - `sampleID`: Unique identifier for each essay - `before`: Text preceding the gap - `after`: Text following the gap - `opt_0`, `opt_1`, `opt_2`, `opt_3`: Four candidate sentences to choose from ## Evaluation Submissions are evaluated using **F1 Score** (macro-averaged across all four classes): $$F1 = 2 \times \frac{\text{Precision} \times \text{Recall}}{\text{Precision} + \text{Recall}}$$ Macro-averaging ensures each option class (0, 1, 2, 3) is weighted equally regardless of frequency in the test set. ## Submission Submit a CSV file: ``` sampleID,answer 100,0 101,1 102,2 103,3 ``` - `sampleID`: ID from test.csv - `answer`: Predicted option index (0, 1, 2, or 3) All test samples must be included with valid indices. --- ## data-description You have access to two files: - **train.csv**: Contains labeled examples with complete sentence mappings - **test.csv**: Contains unlabeled examples where you must predict the correct option `train.csv`: - `sampleID`: Unique identifier for each essay - `before`: Text preceding the gap (lorem ipsum in previews) - `after`: Text following the gap (lorem ipsum in previews) - `opt_0`, `opt_1`, `opt_2`, `opt_3`: Four candidate sentences to fill the gap - `label`: Correct option index (0, 1, 2, or 3) `test.csv`: - `sampleID`: Unique identifier for each essay - `before`: Text preceding the gap - `after`: Text following the gap - `opt_0`, `opt_1`, `opt_2`, `opt_3`: Four candidate sentences to choose from --- ## rules - use of LLMs for writing code or getting task ideas is forbidden - use of internet besides the reading of official library documentations and accessing the contest platform is forbidden - communicating with anyone during the contest is forbidden - only ask questions regarding task statements using the #clarification-requests on the discord server We can't enforce these rules, but following them ensures fair play and a more accurate simulation of the IOAI. It is in the best interest of the contestants they follow the rules. ##ENTRY IN THIS COMPETITION CONSTITUTES YOUR ACCEPTANCE OF THESE OFFICIAL COMPETITION RULES. **[See Section 3.18 for defined terms](rules#18.-terms)** *The Competition named below is a skills-based competition to promote and further the field of data science. You must register via the Competition Website to enter. To enter the Competition, you must agree to these Official Competition Rules, which incorporate by reference the provisions and content of the Competition Website and any Specific Competition Rules herein (collectively, the "Rules"). Please read these Rules carefully before entry to ensure you understand and agree. You further agree that Submission in the Competition constitutes agreement to these Rules. You may not submit to the Competition and are not eligible to receive the prizes associated with this Competition unless you agree to these Rules. These Rules form a binding legal agreement between you and the Competition Sponsor with respect to the Competition. Your competition Submissions must conform to the requirements stated on the Competition Website. Your Submissions will be scored based on the evaluation metric described on the Competition Website. Subject to compliance with the Competition Rules, Prizes, if any, will be awarded to Participants with the best scores, based on the merits of the data science models submitted. See below for the complete Competition Rules.* **You cannot sign up to Kaggle from multiple accounts and therefore you cannot enter or submit from multiple accounts.**

tasks.description: Dataset distillation для MNIST: сгенерировать ровно один синтетический пример — 28×28 grayscale изображение (784 float-пикселя в [0,1]) и 10-мерный soft-label (неотрицательный, сумма ≈ 1). Тренировочный набор — обычный MNIST (60k изображений) только для анализа; фиксированная CNN будет переобучена с нуля на единственном вашем примере и оценена на 1000 скрытых тестовых цифрах. Метрика — Accuracy переобученной модели (бейзлайн 0.05, референс ~0.2). Запрещены LLM для генерации идей/кода и интернет помимо документации.
tasks.full_description:
## abstract

This competition is organized by the [IOAI Community Contest group](https://ioai-community-contest.netlify.app/) (not affiliated with IOAI), which is made up of IOAI alumni. It is meant to prepare students for the IOAI (International Olympiad in Artificial Intelligence).

---

## Description

# 1. Problem Description

A factory produces small electronic devices that each contain an identical image classifier. The classifier is a lightweight convolutional network trained to recognize handwritten digits (0 through 9). Due to hardware constraints, each device can only receive a single training example over its update channel before retraining.

Your job is to design that single training example.

You will craft:
- one **28×28 grayscale image**
- one **10-dimensional soft label vector**

A fixed CNN will then be retrained from scratch using only your example and evaluated on real handwritten digits. The better your synthetic example encodes information about all ten digit classes, the higher the model scores.

---

# 2. Dataset

```text
data/
├── mnist_images/
│   ├── img_00000.png
│   ├── img_00001.png
│   ├── ...
│   └── img_59999.png
└── train_labels.csv
```

The training set is provided for your analysis only. Your submission contains no real images. The hidden test set is drawn from the same distribution.

---

# 3. Task

Produce exactly **one synthetic training sample** consisting of:

- A **28×28 grayscale image**
  - 784 pixel values
  - floats in `[0, 1]`

- A **10-dimensional soft label vector**
  - non-negative values
  - sums to `1.0`

---

## data-description

# Data Description

MNIST Dataset, containing 60k 0-9 digit images, with 28x28 shape.

## Files

- **train_labels.csv** – The training set containing image file paths and corresponding numeric labels.  
- **mnist_images/** – Directory containing all the grayscale images referenced in `train_labels.csv`.

## Columns

- **filepath** – Relative path to the image file (e.g., `mnist_images/img_00000.png`).  
- **label** – Numeric label for the image, ranging from 0 to 9.

---

## Evaluation

## 4. Submission

Submit a CSV with exactly one data row. Columns: `id`, `p0`–`p783` (flattened 28×28 pixel values, floats in [0,1]), and `l0`–`l9` (soft label probabilities, non-negative, sum to ~1.0).

```python
import pandas as pd
row = {'id': 0}
row.update({f'p{i}': pixel_values[i] for i in range(784)})
row.update({f'l{i}': label_probs[i] for i in range(10)})
submission = pd.DataFrame([row])
submission.to_csv('submission.csv', index=False)
```

## 5. Score

Classification accuracy of the retrained model on 1,000 hidden test images.

$$\text{Accuracy} = \frac{\text{Correct Predictions}}{1000}$$

- **Baseline Score:** 0.05
- **Reference Solution Score:** ~0.2

The baseline can be found on the **[Code](https://www.kaggle.com/code/antoningorokva/baseline-extreme-condensation-aicc-round-4)** section of this competition or in Google Colab [Here](https://colab.research.google.com/drive/1MzdYtVWNmpVyjTvvnU0RWw-8F8UyW70B?usp=sharing)

---

## rules

- use of LLMs for writing code or getting task ideas is forbidden
- use of internet besides the reading of official library documentations and accessing the contest platform is forbidden
- communicating with anyone during the contest is forbidden
- For discussing competition details, participants may use the optional `#clarification-requests` channel on the [Discord server](https://discord.com/invite/7GfxrqRreY)

We can't enforce these rules, but following them ensures fair play and a more accurate simulation of the IOAI. It is in the best interest of the contestants they follow the rules.

Датасет — 108 jpg-картинок 15 знаменитостей, train-меток нет. Метрика — средний F1 по 15 запросам; запрещены face-specific модели/библиотеки (FaceNet, ArcFace, dlib и т.п.) и ручная разметка, разрешены general-purpose pretrained (CLIP, ResNet, ViT), лимиты <16 GB VRAM и 30 минут.
tasks.full_description:
## abstract This competition is organized by the **IOAI Community Contest** group (not affiliated with IOAI), which is made up of IOAI alumni. It is meant to **prepare** students for the IOAI (International Olympiad in Artificial Intelligence). Deep inside the North-Pole workshop, Santa’s portrait-elves have painted 100 tiny festive portraits of 15 very special guests—celebrity carolers who will lead the midnight choir. Each guest sat only once, yet their likeness appears again and again among the stacked canvases, hidden by holly, candle-glow, and ribbon. Mrs. Claus hands you a single golden frame: the reference portrait for each of the 15 stars. Your mission, before the sleigh bells ring, is to wander the candlelit gallery and pick out every other canvas that bears the same face, even if a Santa hat or string of lights partly disguises it. Find them all and the album will be complete, ensuring every singer sees themselves reflected in the Christmas Eve concert program. Formally, you are given a collection of 100 images of 15 different celebrities. Your task is to identify which other images in the dataset show the **same celebrity** as a given reference image. You will be provided with: - **`ref_img.csv`**: Contains one reference image ID per celebrity (15 rows total) - **`images/`**: Directory containing all 108 images as `.jpg` files, named with 3-digit IDs (e.g., `000.jpg`, `001.jpg`, etc.) Baseline: https://drive.google.com/file/d/1cj_3KEJamQaJ2cc-lVTjn-VXkxsyLmTN/view?usp=sharing To use the baseline, copy it to your Google Drive. We will also upload soon a notebook on Kaggle's Code section. --- ## Description For each reference image in `ref_img.csv`, you must find all images from the dataset that **show the same celebrity** as the reference image. The reference image itself should **not** be included in your results. Note: Each celebrity appears a variable number of times in the dataset. Constraints: - **No face-specific models**: You cannot use pretrained models specifically designed for face recognition or face feature extraction (e.g., FaceNet, ArcFace, VGGFace, DeepFace) - **General pretrained models allowed**: You may use other general-purpose pretrained models (e.g., CLIP, ResNet, ViT, etc.) - **No face-specific libraries**: You cannot use libraries specifically built for face detection/recognition (e.g., face_recognition, dlib, MediaPipe Face). However you are allowed to use anything included in OpenCV, without additional downloads. - **No manual labeling**: You cannot manually group images and you also cannot manually write text labels for each image - **Memory limit**: Solutions must run with less than 16GB VRAM - **Time limit**: The entire notebook execution must complete in under 30 minutes ## Evaluation Your submission will be evaluated using **F1 Score**, which measures how well you balance finding all matching faces while avoiding false matches. - **F1**: The harmonic mean of precision and recall. A good F1 means you found most of the matching faces without making too many mistakes. The final score is the average F1 across all 15 reference images. ## Submission Format Create a `submission.csv` file with the following format: | Column | Description | | --------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------------- | | `ref_img` | 3-digit ID of the reference image | | `photos` | Pipe-separated (`\|`) list of image IDs that match the reference image | **Example:** ``` ref_img,photos 042,001|002|003 089,001|002 031,001|002|003 ``` **Important:** - Each submission must have exactly 15 rows (one per reference image) - Do not include the reference image in your predictions - IDs must be formatted as 3-digit strings (e.g., `005` not `5`) - Each celebrity has at least 5 photos --- ## data-description You will be provided with: - **`ref_img.csv`**: Contains one reference image ID per celebrity (15 rows total) - **`images/`**: Directory containing all 108 images as `.jpg` files, named with 3-digit IDs (e.g., `000.jpg`, `001.jpg`, etc.) For each reference image in `ref_img.csv`, you must find all images from the dataset that **show the same celebrity** as the reference image. The reference image itself should **not** be included in your results. --- ## rules - use of LLMs for writing code or getting task ideas is forbidden - use of internet besides the reading of official library documentations and accessing the contest platform is forbidden - communicating with anyone during the contest is forbidden - only ask questions regarding task statements using the #clarification-requests on the discord server We can't enforce these rules, but following them ensures fair play and a more accurate simulation of the IOAI. It is in the best interest of the contestants they follow the rules. ##ENTRY IN THIS COMPETITION CONSTITUTES YOUR ACCEPTANCE OF THESE OFFICIAL COMPETITION RULES. **[See Section 3.18 for defined terms](rules#18.-terms)** *The Competition named below is a skills-based competition to promote and further the field of data science. tasks.full_description:
## abstract

This competition is organized by the **IOAI Community Contest** group (not affiliated with IOAI), which is made up of IOAI alumni.  
It is meant to prepare students for the **IOAI (International Olympiad in Artificial Intelligence)**.  
Information about the task can be found below and the `baseline.ipynb` file is in the "Data" and "Code" sections.

---

## Task

## Problem Statement
You are given a collection of short audio clips assembled from public speech datasets. Each clip is assigned an **anonymized class label** (labels were remapped to dataset-specific anonymized IDs). Audio quality, loudness, background noise, speaker accent, and recording conditions may vary between clips because sources are heterogeneous.

Your goal is to build a model that predicts the anonymized class label for each audio clip using only the provided training data. This is a closed-set classification problem: every test example belongs to one of the classes present in the training set.


---

## Dataset Description

### 1. `train.csv`
Metadata for the training portion of the dataset.

**Columns:**
- `path` — relative file path to the `.wav` audio clip  
- `label` — target language label (may include noise or mislabels)

The audio files referenced here are stored in the dataset under the paths indicated.

---

### 2. `test.csv`
Metadata for the test portion of the dataset.

**Columns:**
- `ID` — unique integer identifier  
- `path` — relative file path to the `.wav` audio clip for which a prediction is required  

During the competition, the true labels for these clips are hidden.

---

### 3. `solution.csv` (hidden)
Used internally for scoring.

**Columns:**
- `ID` — matches IDs in `test.csv`  
- `label` — true language label for that test clip  

Participants do **not** have access to this file.

---

### 4. Audio Files
All audio data is provided as `.wav` files referenced by `path` in the metadata.  
Sampling rates, durations, and volume levels may vary between clips.

---

# Output Format

Participants must submit a CSV file containing:

- `ID` — matching IDs in `test.csv`  
- `label` — the predicted language label for each audio clip

---

## data-description

Here you can find the datasets provided to the contestants

---

## rules

- use of LLMs for writing code or getting task ideas is **forbidden**
- use of internet besides the reading of official library documentations and accessing the contest platform is **forbidden**
- communicating with anyone during the contest is **forbidden**
- only ask questions regarding task statements using the #clarification-requests on the [discord server](https://discord.com/invite/7GfxrqRreY)

We can't enforce these rules, but following them ensures fair play and a more accurate simulation of the IOAI. It is in the best interest of the contestants they follow the rules.

tasks.description: Unsupervised бинарная классификация: определить, какая из двух скрытых моделей (A или B) сгенерировала наблюдаемые 5-мерные логиты по 100-мерному входу. Train: 5000 строк с X (100D) и Y (5D) без меток; выданы веса двух penultimate-модулей. Метрика — Accuracy. Источник логитов в трейне неизвестен, истинной разметки нет.
tasks.full_description:
## abstract This competition is organized by the **IOAI Community Contest** group (not affiliated with IOAI), which is made up of IOAI alumni. It is meant to prepare students for the **IOAI (International Olympiad in Artificial Intelligence)**. Information about the task can be found below and the `baseline.ipynb` file is in the "Data" and "Code" sections. --- ## Task # Task Description ## Problem Statement You are given a dataset where each sample’s 5-D logits `Y` were produced by **one of two hidden models (A or B)**. Both models map 100-D inputs to 10-D embeddings, but with different learned transformations, and then (in the real system) apply a **hidden final linear head** to produce `Y`. Participants are provided with: - The **two models up to (but not including) the final layer** (i.e., penultimate modules for A and B), - The actual **inputs** `X` (100-D), - The observed **outputs** `Y` (5-D logits). The true source label (which model generated each `Y`) is **not** provided. Your task is to predict, for each row, **which model (A or B) generated the logits**. --- ## Input Format - `train.csv`: Training data available to participants. **Columns**: - `ID` - unique identifier for each row - `X_1 … X_100` - 100-D input vector - `Y_1 … Y_5` - 5-D observed logits (produced by *either* Model A or Model B) - `modelA_penultimate.pth`, `modelB_penultimate.pth`: Serialized weights for the two models **up to the 10-D penultimate layer** (final linear head is missing). *Participants can load these modules and compute embeddings `ZA = f_A(X)` and `ZB = f_B(X)` themselves.* - `solution.csv` (hidden): Ground-truth source labels for the test set. **Columns**: - `ID` - matches IDs in `test.csv` - `Source` - `"A"` or `"B"` indicating which model generated the logits > **Note:** No file with source labels is provided for training. The problem is intentionally unlabeled. --- ## Output Format Submit a CSV file `submission.csv` with: - `ID` - must match `test.csv` - `Source` - your predicted label for each row (`"A"` or `"B"`) --- # Scoring Method Submissions are evaluated using the **Accuracy** between the predicted class labels and the teacher-only class labels: $$ \mathrm{Accuracy} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} \mathbf{1}\left(y_i = \hat{y}_i\right) $$ Where:
  • \(y_i\) — true teacher class label from solution.csv
  • \(\hat{y}_i\) — predicted class label from the participant submission
  • \(n\) — number of samples in the test set
--- ## data-description Here you can find the datasets provided to the contestants and the baseline --- ## rules - use of LLMs for writing code or getting task ideas is **forbidden** - use of internet besides the reading of official library documentations and accessing the contest platform is **forbidden** - communicating with anyone during the contest is **forbidden** - only ask questions regarding task statements using the #clarification-requests on the discord server We can't enforce these rules, but following them ensures fair play and a more accurate simulation of the IOAI. It is in the best interest of the contestants they follow the rules.

## abstract

This competition is organized by the [IOAI Community Contest](https://ioai-community-contest.netlify.app/) group (not affiliated with IOAI), which is made up of IOAI alumni. It is meant to **prepare** students for the IOAI (International Olympiad in Artificial Intelligence).

---

## Task

# Lost Interpreter
## Task statement
A good friend of yours, Tadhg, was able to finish his first programming project, a simple assembly language interpreter, and was planning to present it as his end-of-year project. His sister, Aoibheann, however, accidentally deleted the entire interpreter while trying to install a Minecraft mod on his computer. 

Tadhg was really sad but he remembered that he had saved several samples of the language along with their outputs. He sent those to you and went completely off the grid. Unfortunately, he is the only person that knows the API of the language. The first demonstration is due Sunday night, and he needs a good enough prototype. Your task is to build an interpreter for your good friend.

## Formal Specification
You must implement a function that, given a program as input, simulates its execution and returns the final value of the variable after the program terminates.

You analyze the language and you make some **true** observations:

1. A program is a sequence of instructions separated by `;`.  

2. Each instruction has the form with `n` being a natural number:
    ```
    OPCODE OPERAND1 OPERAND2 ... OPERANDN
    ```

3. The language uses a single variable, which is the output, and all instructions can operate on it.

4. Some instructions may alter the **execution order** (control flow), 
   such as jumping to another instruction instead of continuing sequentially.

For example:
```
OP1 5 -3 ; OP3 3 ; OP3 0 1 2 3 ; OP4 2
```
### ⚠️ Important Note
> You must **not** use any pretrained models

## Metric
The evaluation metric is **Mean Absolute Error (MAE)**.

For each prediction, MAE measures the absolute difference between the predicted output and the true output. The final score is the average of these absolute differences over all test examples.

Lower MAE is better.

## Dataset
You are given a `train.csv` with two columns:
1. `program`, string of the programs
2. `output`, the resulting variable after the program executes

You are also given `test.csv` with one column:
1. `ID` that **should not** be altered
2. `program`, string of a program

## Submission
You need to submit the corresponding outputs of `test.csv` in a csv with columns:
1. `ID`, matching the IDs from `test.csv` (must not be altered)
2. `output`, the final value of the variable after executing the given program

Each row in your submission should correspond to a row in `test.csv`.

---

## data-description

You are given a `train.csv` with two columns:
1. `program`, string of the programs
2. `output`, the resulting variable after the program executes

You are also given `test.csv` with one column:
1. `ID` that **should not** be altered
2. `program`, string of a program

---

## rules

- Use of LLMs for writing code or getting task ideas is forbidden
- Use of internet besides the reading of official library documentations and accessing the contest platform is forbidden
- Communicating with anyone during the contest is forbidden
- For discussing competition details, participants may use the optional ```#clarification-requests``` channel on the [Discord server
](https://discord.com/invite/7GfxrqRreY)

We can't enforce these rules, but following them ensures fair play and a more accurate simulation of the IOAI. It is in the best interest of the contestants they follow the rules.

Train ~7401 строк по 1434 фичи Gene1-Gene1434 плюс batch; формат — 9 файлов RNA_seq_patient_0..8 и test.csv. Метрика — macro-averaged F1 Score (baseline 0.2413). ## abstract

This competition is organized by the [IOAI Community Contest](https://aicc-official.org) group (not affiliated with IOAI), which is made up of IOAI alumni. It is meant to prepare students for the IOAI (International Olympiad in Artificial Intelligence).  

The baseline notebook can be found in the 'Data' tab, as well as on Colab [here](https://colab.research.google.com/drive/1F3v_f1nzEtjHdEwm3RSG4c54QlJce5NV?usp=sharing).  

NOTE: Reference solution score ~~will be updated on the leaderboard within the next 24 hours~~ may not be available until after the competition, as the problem author is currently unreachable. Apologies for the inconvenience.

---

## Description

Train a classifier that can classify cells into one of 12 types, based on the expression level of **1434** genes.  

Note that the dataset is very large. You may run into issues with RAM usage if you do not manage your memory properly. Remember to `del` your unused variables!  

### Output Format

You must output as CSV file with two columns:
- `id`: The index of the predicted label, according to `test.csv` (equivalent to its index within `test_df`)  
- `label`: The predicted label for the particular row of data.

---

### Scoring Method

The performance of your model will be determined by **macro-averaged F1 Score**.  
The baseline solution in this notebook scores **0.2413**, rounded to 4 decimal places.

---

### Architecture Restrictions

There are no architectural restrictions, apart from the standard 'no LLM' rule. Good luck!

---

## data-description

You are given 10 files:
- `RNA_seq_patient_0`-`RNA_seq_patient_8`: These 9 files were taken from **9 seperate patients**, and will be the training data for your model. Each row has the features `Gene1`-`Gene1434` (containing the expression level of each gene), `batch` (the patient the data was taken from) and `label` (the target cell type from 1-12).
- `test.csv`: This is the test set, taken from a separate 10th patient. It contains the features `Gene1`-`Gene1434` and `batch` only.

---

## rules

- Use of LLMs for writing code or getting task ideas is forbidden
- Use of internet besides the reading of official library documentations and accessing the contest platform is forbidden
communicating with anyone during the contest is forbidden
- For discussing competition details, participants may use the optional `#clarification-requests` channel on the Discord server

We can't enforce these rules, but following them ensures fair play and a more accurate simulation of the IOAI. It is in the best interest of the contestants they follow the rules.  

You may not republish this task, or any part of its data, without permission from the authors.

---

## Storyline

You burst through the front doors of the AICC hospital. Checking your watch - *2:34 pm* - you breathe a sigh of relief. You're not late for your appraisal.  

Waiting in line to see the CEM, you see as engineers walk out aghast, faces pale as ghosts. Before long, a familiar, dreaded voice rings out. "Mr Dude, C., please proceed into the meeting room."

You walk in. A smartly dressed avatar, looking very much like a doctor, appears through a hologram. A virtual nameplate says: `Dr. GBT VII, Chief Executive Model`.  

`Good morning ☀️ You did an amazing job on your previous task — 79.5% accuracy is seriously impressive. Keep it up, you’re doing great.`

You glance at your wrist briefly. *No wonder we're still in the red.*  

`…However, I need to be honest with you — one of the patients from the missed cases has decided to sue us, and I’m in big trouble because of it.`  

*Welp, I've always wanted to get into farming. It's pretty a chill job.*  

`But there may be an opportunity coming up soon that could shift how all of this is being viewed.`

Your eyes widen.  

`It's something along the lines of figuring out a complete cure for this 'cancer' bug in human genes. Nothing urgent, just… whenever you get a moment.`  

*...what?*  

`I’ll consolidate the relevant materials and circle back by sending the data to your email once the qubits are aligned.` The avatar fizzles out.  

You stare blankly at where the avatar used to be. Perhaps this is why the other engineers looked so concerned.  

*Quite a massive problem...* you think to yourself. *But nothing a bit of AI can't fix, right?*

## abstract

This competition is organized by the [IOAI Community Contest group](https://ioai-community-contest.netlify.app/) (not affiliated with IOAI), which is made up of IOAI alumni. It is meant to prepare students for the IOAI (International Olympiad in Artificial Intelligence).

---

## Description

## 1. Problem Description

**Wubba Lubba Dub Dub\!** You've stepped into the shoes of Morty Smith. While on an adventure to the Narnia Dimension, where time fractures into 5 possible timelines, your friend Rick got drunk and passed out, leaving you stranded. To get back home, you need to synchronize your portal gun with the correct timeline.

Fortunately, Rick has 8 alien watches strapped to his wrist, which he bought from a sketchy merchant. Unfortunately, they each follow some bizarre, unknown logic.

Along with the watches, the merchant included a worn notebook containing some past watch readings he had recorded. Each entry lists 4 candidate timelines, but only one is correct. The rest are decoys.

Using the merchant's notebook, your goal is to decode the pattern behind the watches and determine the correct timeline for each reading.

![Rick with 8 watches](https://i.imgur.com/uuhtBFM.png)

## 2. Dataset

The dataset consists of watch readings and candidate labels.

### Files
- **`train.csv`**: Contains `row_id`, features `x0`...`x7`, and candidate flags `cand_0`...`cand_4`.
- **`test.csv`**: Contains `row_id` and features `x0`...`x7` only.

### Features (`x0` ... `x7`)
- 8 continuous float values representing the position of each watch hand.
- **Important:** These features are **cyclical**. Think of a clock: 11:59 is closer to 12:01 than it is to 6:00. Similarly, for a watch with period T, a value near 0 is adjacent to a value near T.

### Candidates (`cand_0` ... `cand_4`)
- Found only in `train.csv`.
- Each column indicates whether that timeline is a candidate (1) or impossible (0) for that reading.
- For every row, exactly 4 columns are set to 1. The true label is always one of them.

### Constraint
Across all the data (train and test), each of the 5 timelines occurs exactly 500 times in total.

The label distribution may differ between train and test. Using the test set during development is allowed.

## 3. Task

Your task is to predict the 1 correct label (0, 1, 2, 3, or 4) for each row in `test.csv` based on the 8 cyclical features (`x0`...`x7`).

---

## data-description

The dataset consists of watch readings and candidate labels.

### Files
- **`train.csv`**: Contains `row_id`, features `x0`...`x7`, and candidate flags `cand_0`...`cand_4`.
- **`test.csv`**: Contains `row_id` and features `x0`...`x7` only.

### Features (`x0` ... `x7`)
- 8 continuous float values representing the position of each watch hand.
- **Important:** These features are **cyclical**. Think of a clock: 11:59 is closer to 12:01 than it is to 6:00. Similarly, for a watch with period T, a value near 0 is adjacent to a value near T.

### Candidates (`cand_0` ... `cand_4`)
- Found only in `train.csv`.
- Each column indicates whether that timeline is a candidate (1) or impossible (0) for that reading.
- For every row, exactly 4 columns are set to 1. The true label is always one of them.

### Constraint
Across all the data (train and test), each of the 5 timelines occurs exactly 500 times in total.

The label distribution may differ between train and test. Using the test set during development is allowed.

---

## Evaluation

## 4. Submission

Submit a CSV file with your predictions for the test set in the following format:
```csv
row_id,label
0,2
1,4
...
```

Example code to generate submission:
```python
import pandas as pd

submission = pd.DataFrame({
    'row_id': test['row_id'],
    'label': y_pred  # your predictions
})
submission.to_csv('submission.csv', index=False)
```

## 5. Score
Your submission is evaluated using **Accuracy**.
$$ \text{Accuracy} = \frac{\text{Number of Correct Predictions}}{\text{Total Number of Test Samples}} $$

*   **Baseline Score:** ~0.17
*   **Reference Solution Score:** ~0.99

The baseline can be accessed in the Code section of the competition ([pinned notebook](https://www.kaggle.com/code/antoningorokva/baseline-morty-s-time-paradox-aicc-round-3)) or via [Google Colab](https://colab.research.google.com/drive/177ohW95qs65jgeAYmwaK-B0Jc-wBLfze?usp=sharing).

---

## rules

- use of internet besides the reading of official library documentations and accessing the contest platform is forbidden
- communicating with anyone during the contest is forbidden
- For discussing competition details, participants may use the optional `#clarification-requests` channel on the [Discord server](https://discord.com/invite/7GfxrqRreY)

Train: пары RGB-JPEG + PNG-маски разных размеров и органов; test: 42 RGB-изображения без масок, ответ — base85-PNG маска того же размера. Метрика — IoU/Dice-подобный шаблон. Ограничения: pretrained-модели только из torchvision, запрещены внешние датасеты, обучение на test/val (в т. ч. unsupervised и псевдо-разметка через CLIP), LLM для кода и интернет.
tasks.full_description:
## abstract

This competition is organized by the [**AI Community Contest**](http://aicc-official.org/) group, which is made up of IOAI alumni. It is meant to **prepare** students for the IOAI (International Olympiad in Artificial Intelligence).

The baseline can be found on Colab [here](https://colab.research.google.com/drive/16Nd5ANx9RCikfZNOLt8wKlEjhPfUmK6W)

---

## Description

Accurate identification of cell nuclei in microscopic tissue images is a cornerstone of modern computational pathology. Pathologists routinely examine Hematoxylin and Eosin (H&E) stained slides to diagnose diseases, assess tumor grade, and guide treatment decisions. Automating nuclei segmentation can dramatically accelerate this process, enabling large-scale quantitative analysis of tissue microenvironments. However, the task is challenging: staining intensity varies across labs and scanners, nuclei overlap and touch, and some cells are faint or irregularly shaped. A robust algorithm must see beyond raw color and understand the underlying tissue physics.

You are given a collection of H&E-stained histopathology images. For each test image, your goal is to produce a **binary segmentation mask** where white pixels (`255`) represent nuclei and black pixels (`0`) represent background. Your model must learn this mapping from the training images and their corresponding ground-truth masks.

The answer for each test image must be encoded as a **base85 PNG string** and submitted inside a single `.csv` file.

# Dataset Description

The data is derived from the **MoNuSAC** (Multi-Organ Nuclei Segmentation and Classification) dataset. Original whole-slide images were annotated by expert pathologists using polygonal region boundaries around individual nuclei. These annotations were rasterized into binary masks for this task.

- **Training data**: RGB JPEG images alongside their corresponding PNG masks (`{id}.jpg` and `{id}_mask.png`).
- **Test data**: RGB JPEG images without masks.
- Images vary in size and come from multiple organs and scanners, meaning color appearance, nuclear size, and tissue density vary significantly across the dataset.

**Note**: images have varied sizes and aspect ratios, the output mask must have the same size as the input image.

## Domain specific knowledge

You may use these insights for more advanced feature engineering and model design; however, it's fully optional to use them.

1. **H&E Staining Physics and Color Deconvolution**

H&E slides are not "natural images." The purple color comes from Hematoxylin binding to nucleic acids (highlighting nuclei), while the pink comes from Eosin binding to cytoplasmic proteins. Because the physical absorption of these two dyes is well understood, you can mathematically separate an RGB image into independent Hematoxylin and Eosin channels using **color deconvolution**. This gives the network a direct "nuclei channel" to learn from instead of forcing it to infer staining chemistry from raw RGB correlations. Sample NumPy implementation:

```py
def deconvolve_he_numpy(image):
    # Ruifrok & Johnston reference absorption vectors
    stain_matrix = np.array([
        [0.644211, 0.716556, 0.266844],  # Hematoxylin
        [0.092789, 0.954111, 0.283111],  # Eosin
    ])
    stain_matrix /= np.linalg.norm(stain_matrix, axis=1, keepdims=True)

    # Convert to Optical Density
    od = -np.log((image.astype(np.float64) + 1.0) / 255.0)
    od_flat = od.reshape((-1, 3))

    # Deconvolve via pseudo-inverse
    concentrations = (np.linalg.pinv(stain_matrix.T) @ od_flat.T).T
    h_channel = concentrations[:, 0].reshape(image.shape[:2])
    e_channel = concentrations[:, 1].reshape(image.shape[:2])
    return h_channel, e_channel
```

2. **Optical Density and Stain Normalization**
  
Under the Beer–Lambert law, light transmission through stained tissue follows an exponential decay. Transforming RGB to **Optical Density (OD)** space makes the stains linearly separable. Because scanner settings and lab protocols vary, a model trained on "red-ish" slides may fail on "blue-ish" ones. **Macenko stain normalization** uses SVD on the OD image to find the dominant stain vectors for that specific slide, normalizes the concentrations to a reference standard, and reconstructs a standardized RGB image. Applying this to both training and test images dramatically reduces domain shift. Sample NumPy implementation:

```py
def macenko_normalize(img, alpha=1.0, beta=0.15):
    # Reference H&E vectors and max concentrations (Macenko et al.)
    ref_he = np.array([
        [0.5626, 0.7201, 0.4062],
        [0.2159, 0.8012, 0.5581],
    ])
    ref_he /= np.linalg.norm(ref_he, axis=1, keepdims=True)
    ref_max_c = np.array([1.9705, 1.0308])

    # 1. Optical Density
    img = np.maximum(img.astype(np.float64), 1.0)
    od = -np.log(img / 255.0).reshape(-1, 3)

    # 2. Keep only stained pixels
    mask = (od >= beta).any(axis=1)
    if mask.sum() < 10:
        return img.astype(np.uint8).reshape(img.shape)

    # 3. SVD to find dominant stain directions
    _, _, vh = np.linalg.svd(od[mask], full_matrices=False)
    v = vh[:2].T

    # 4. Extreme angles on the projection plane
    proj = od[mask] @ v
    phi = np.arctan2(proj[:, 1], proj[:, 0])
    phi_min, phi_max = np.percentile(phi, [alpha, 100 - alpha])

    # 5. Stain vectors for this slide
    stain_vectors = v @ np.array([
        [np.cos(phi_min), np.cos(phi_max)],
        [np.sin(phi_min), np.sin(phi_max)],
    ])
    # Hematoxylin is bluer (higher 3rd component)
    stain_vectors = np.stack(sorted(stain_vectors.T, key=lambda vec: vec[2], reverse=True))

    # 6. Concentrations and normalization
    concentrations = (np.linalg.pinv(stain_vectors.T) @ od.T).T
    concentrations = concentrations / (np.percentile(concentrations, 99, axis=0) + 1e-8)
    concentrations = concentrations * ref_max_c

    # 7. Reconstruct standardized RGB
    normalized_od = (ref_he.T @ concentrations.T).T
    normalized = np.exp(-normalized_od) * 255.0
    return np.clip(normalized, 0, 255).astype(np.uint8).reshape(img.shape)
```

3. **Adaptive Contrast Enhancement (CLAHE)**

Nuclei in some regions may be faint, out of focus, or unevenly illuminated due to tissue folding. **Contrast Limited Adaptive Histogram Equalization (CLAHE)** operates on small tiles of the image and enhances local contrast without amplifying noise globally. Applying CLAHE to the Hematoxylin channel before feature extraction can reveal weak or partially faded nuclei that would otherwise be invisible to the model. CLAHE can be applied with OpenCV.

# Output Format

The output file (`.csv`) must contain exactly 3 columns:

| subtaskID                                         | datapointID                                                             | answer                                                   |
| ------------------------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------- |
| The subtask identifier (always `1` for this task) | The random ID extracted from the test image filename (e.g., `a3f9d2e1`) | A base85-encoded PNG string of the predicted binary mask |

**Important:** The decoded mask must be a single-channel grayscale PNG where nuclei are `255` and background is `0`. The mask must match the original dimensions of the test image before encoding.

You must submit only one `.csv` file containing all predictions.

# Scoring

Scoring is based on **Mean Intersection over Union (IoU)** between your predicted masks and the ground-truth masks.

$$
\text{IoU} = \frac{|\text{Pred} \cap \text{GT}|}{|\text{Pred} \cup \text{GT}|}
$$

Per-image IoU is computed on the foreground class (`255`), and the final score is the average over all test images. Failed submissions (e.g., missing rows, decoding errors, or malformed CSV) receive 0 points.

---

## data-description

The data is derived from the **MoNuSAC** (Multi-Organ Nuclei Segmentation and Classification) dataset. Original whole-slide images were annotated by expert pathologists using polygonal region boundaries around individual nuclei. These annotations were rasterized into binary masks for this task.

- **Training data**: RGB JPEG images alongside their corresponding PNG masks (`{id}.jpg` and `{id}_mask.png`).
- **Test data**: RGB JPEG images without masks.
- Images vary in size and come from multiple organs and scanners, meaning color appearance, nuclear size, and tissue density vary significantly across the dataset.

---

## rules

## Task-Specific Architecture Restrictions

1. You **may not** use any pre-trained models, apart from those provided in [torchvision](https://docs.pytorch.org/vision/main/models.html).
2. You **may not** train your model directly on the validation or test samples in any way. This includes any unsupervised learning technique, calculating statistics over them, running GPT-5 agents etc.
3. You **may not** use any external dataset.
4. You **may not** label the unlabeled sketches or test set with any pre-trained model, e.g. CLIP.

## AICC Contest Rules

- The use of LLMs for writing code or getting task ideas is forbidden.
- Use of the internet, besides the reading of official library documentation and accessing the contest platform, is forbidden.
- Communicating with anyone during the contest is forbidden.
- Only ask questions regarding task statements using the #clarification-requests on the discord server.

We can't enforce these rules, but following them ensures fair play and a more accurate simulation of the IOAI. It is in the best interest of the contestants they follow the rules.
